title: practical english usage - Part 68
content:
A  negative interrogative form Hadn't.. . better...? is possible.

me... is possible in British English but very unusual.) You'd better not wake me up when you come in. (You hadn't better wake We   normally make the negative with had better not + infinitive.

beUer-hufryifig-r-iedHHfy...) not                                                    or It's late - you had better hurry up. (     .. .you have better...     ... you had better is impossible). After had better we  use the infinitive without to.

Had better refers to the immediate future, but the form is always past (have 2 forms Section 7 Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  80  instructions and requests: will, would, can, could, might, shall Could you lend me £5  until tomorrow?

Can you put the children to bed?

direct and more polite.

Can and could are used to give instructions or make requests. Could is less If you would come this way...

If you'll just wait here for a moment, I'll see if Mr Andrews is free.

If you will/would... can be used in polite requests.

Would you open a window, please?

Will you be quiet, please! Make me a cup of coffee, will you?

Will you get me ɑ  newspaper while you're out?

and more polite.

We   can use will you ...? to tell people to do things.  Would you ...? is less direct might 1  instructions,        requests       and   suggestions:        will,    would,       can,    could, could,      might,      shall 80 instructions         and   requests:      will,    would,       can, I could fix your bicycle for you, if that would help.

Could is possible if we  want an offer to sound less definite.

'I can babysit for you this evening if you like.' 'No, it's all right, thanks.' ’ Can I carry your bag? (= Would  you like me to ...) 'Oh, thank you.' We   often use can when we  offer to do things for people.

4 offers:    Can   I carry      your      bag?

You shall have all you wish for. He shall regret this.

promises and threats. This is now very unusual.

In older English, shall was often used with second- and third-person subjects in I shall give you a teddy bear for your birthday.

common than will.

Shall is also possible in British English after I and we, but it is much less I'll hit you if you do that again.   You'll suffer for this!

I'll phone you tonight, (     J phone ■. . .) not I promise I won't smoke again, (     I prom tse-l-don^f-smoke-...) possible in these cases.

We often use will/'ll in promises and threats. Note that the simple present is not 3  promises       and   threats:     I'll phone       you     tonight.

I don't care what you say -1 won't do it! The car won't start.

Will not or won't is used to refuse, or to talk about refusals.

2  refusals:     I won't      do   it!

I will stop smoking! I really will!

Stressed will can express determination.

I've made my decision and I'm sticking to it.

Well, we've agreed on a price, and I'm going to buy it.

to ... or the present progressive (►  35-36).

To  announce decisions that have already been made, we  generally prefer going grammar •  81   permission: can, could, may, might, be allowed to I wonder if I might have a little more cheese?

May I switch on the TV?

and formal, and is mostly used in indirect question structures.

They  are more formal than can/could, and are less common. Might is very polite May and might are also used to ask for permission, and may to give permission.

'Could I ask you something?’ 'Yes, of course you can.’ (     ... of course you natural in asking for permission than in giving it).

We   do not use could to give or refuse permission (it suggests respect, so is more We   also use could to ask for permission; it is more polite or formal than can.

'Can I have some more cake?' 'No, I'm afraid you can't.' the refusal).

Cannot/can't are used to refuse permission (often with other words to soften You can go now if you want to.

'Can I ask you something?' 'Yes, of course you can.' We   use can to ask for and give permission.

be   allowed         to 81  permission:       can,    could,      may,     might, She might have warned us she was going to stay out all night.
